Their hope that will thus tame the tiger reaction and consolidate reform before renewing it. Others, like the most reform-minded his inner circle, Eduard Shevardnadze and Aleksandr Yakovlev, warn impending dictatorship. Yet even they have warm words for him.
Mr. Gorbachev's reformist critics fall into two camps. One composed radicals who favor the breakup the Soviet Union. They, along with those who cheer them from outside, call for Mr. Gorbachev's overthrow. But they've always underestimated his radicalism and his perseverance, while overestimating their own support.
The other camp consists reformers who have grown disenchanted with the pace change. They fear that has been co-opted apparatchiks who identify freer speech and markets with chaos.
But these critics not include Mr. Shevardnadze Mr. Yakovlev. The real enemy, they still say, not Mr. Gorbachev but the forces reaction deeply entrenched Moscow's ministries, the security forces, Communist Party apparatus and regional military commands.
"We are witnessing the rise vengeful and merciless conservative wave," Mr. Yakovlev said last week. "I'm extremely worried the indifference and fatigue the democratic forces."
Now both and Mr. Shevardnadze are the outside looking in. And both may have lost power what now the central issue confronting Mr. Gorbachev how reconstitute the Union.

The great fear not only Moscow but also Washington that Mr. Gorbachev's alliance convenience will restore the reactionaries and torpedo reform. That could happen.

Mr. Gorbachev knows all that, and more: knows that technological accomplishment cannot proceed without freer discussion and that social renewal cannot come without relaxing central command. For now, and long the transformation the Soviet Union continues, still warrants America's watchful encouragement.
